raw_consumer_statement:
On XXXX, I purchased an extended auto warranty from a company named XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX for {$3000.00} using my American Express card. At the time, I was under significant stressgoing through a divorce and selling my homeand believed I was making a sound decision to protect my vehicle. 

This year, when I finally tried to use the warranty, I found that the company was completely unresponsive. The phone number provided went straight to a continuous busy signal and never connected. Over the past XXXX months, I repeatedly tried calling and emailing, but the email address bounced back, and I found no current contact information online. Their website is defunct, suggesting the company has disappeared entirely. 

I later discovered numerous consumer reports online alleging that XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX is a scam. Their XXXX XXXX XXXX profile shows over XXXX complaints and an XXXX  rating, citing multiple accounts of customers being unable to reach them or receive service. Additional reviews on XXXX, XXXX, and ConsumerAffairs paint a consistent picture : customers paid large sums, could not file claims, and were left with dead phone lines. One reviewer explicitly named the salesman who sold the policy to meColinGreenidentifying him in relation to fraudulent activity. 

On XXXX, I contacted American Express and explained that this was not merely a delayed buyers remorse situation, but a case of fraud. I provided all documentation, including the receipt, the warranty agreement, and records of my attempts to contact the company. I requested that AMEX open a dispute or escalate the case for internal review based on fraud and deceptive practices. The representative refused, stating it was beyond their dispute window. 

On XXXX, I followed up again with AMEX XXXX reiterating the fraudulent nature of the charge, the inaccessibility of the company, and the evidence of misconduct. They again refused to review or escalate the dispute. 

I am now out over {$3000.00} for a product that never existed functionally, sold through deceptive and fraudulent means. AMEXs reluctance to act has left me without recourse. I believe this constitutes a failure of returntoissuer protections and a breach of consumer protection obligations. I am requesting that CFPB review this as a case of card-not-present fraud and misrepresentation, not a standard disputed purchase, and that AMEX be required to reconsider its decision in light of the overwhelming evidence.
